behaviors.description: Cron parser rejects invalid expressions (month 13, day 32, malformed syntax) with descriptive error message
behaviors.justification: This is pure parsing logic with no I/O or external dependencies. Failures are caught by calling the parser function with invalid strings and asserting the correct error type and message content. Mocking would not hide the bug; the parsing logic runs entirely in-process.
behaviors.test_description: Test parser with invalid inputs: parse('13 * * * *') → assert ParsingError mentions 'invalid month'; parse('* * 32 * *') → assert error mentions 'invalid day'; parse('* *') → assert error mentions 'malformed syntax'. Verify error messages are descriptive enough to guide users.

behaviors.description: Next-run-time calculator returns correct timestamps for standard cron expressions, including edge cases like 'last day of month' and 'nearest weekday'
behaviors.justification: Timestamp calculation is deterministic given fixed inputs (cron expression + start time). Can be tested with frozen time to control the calendar, no I/O needed. Pure computation against precomputed reference values. Mocking datetime would not hide the algorithm bug; using real frozen time surfaces calculation errors.
behaviors.test_description: Use freezegun to freeze time. Test standard case: cron '0 2 * * *' from 2024-03-01 09:00 yields 2024-03-01 14:00 UTC. Test edge case: '0 2 L * *' (last day of month) from 2024-02-15 yields 2024-02-29 02:00 (leap year). Test 'nearest weekday': '0 2 ? * 5L' (last Friday) from 2024-01-01 yields 2024-01-26 02:00. Assert exact timestamps.

behaviors.description: Overlap detector identifies when two schedules fire within configured minimum gap (e.g., 5 minutes) and reports conflicting time windows
behaviors.justification: Core logic is comparing computed run timestamps of two schedules against a threshold. Can test by constructing Schedule objects with known next-run times and asserting overlap detection and conflict report format. No persistence, no external systems. A mock of the next-run-time calculator could hide bugs only if the mock is incorrect; testing with real calculated times avoids this.
behaviors.test_description: Create two schedules: sched1 fires at {10:00, 10:05, 10:10, ...}, sched2 fires at {10:02, 10:07, 10:12, ...} with min_gap=5 minutes. Call detector with both schedules. Assert it returns a conflict object identifying the window 10:00–10:05 as conflicting (sched2's 10:02 is within 5 min of sched1's 10:00). Test non-conflicting schedules (gap > 5 min) return no conflicts.

behaviors.description: Timezone-aware scheduling produces correct run times across DST transitions—job scheduled for 2:30 AM handles spring-forward gap and fall-back ambiguity correctly
behaviors.justification: While DST rules depend on the system's timezone database (zoneinfo), behavior is testable at Unit level using real ZoneInfo objects from Python's stdlib. No external API calls or subprocess needed; the timezone database is static and locally available. The challenge is that DST edge cases (non-existent times, ambiguous times) are subtle, so tests must be thorough. Mocking timezone behavior would absolutely hide real bugs; real zoneinfo objects are essential.
behaviors.test_description:
Use ZoneInfo('America/New_York'). Test cron '30 2 * * *' (daily at 2:30 AM) across DST boundaries: 
  - Spring forward (2024-03-10): 2:30 AM doesn't exist (gap 2:00–3:00). Assert scheduler either skips this day or rounds forward to 3:30 AM, applied consistently. 
  - Fall back (2024-11-03): 2:30 AM is ambiguous. Assert scheduler picks the same interpretation (e.g., always first 2:30, or always second 2:30) deterministically.

behaviors.description: When a scheduled job's next run time has passed (system was down), engine determines whether to skip or backfill based on configured missed-run policy
behaviors.justification: Behavior requires interaction between engine and job state (last run time, policy config) and state mutation (creating backfill tasks or marking as skipped). While the *policy logic* ('if time is past AND policy==SKIP then skip') could be unit-tested in isolation, testing the full engine behavior requires a real or in-memory job store. Mocking the persistence layer would hide race conditions, transaction issues, or incorrect cascading updates. Integration test with a real (in-memory or ephemeral) job store is needed.
behaviors.test_description: Set up a job with missed_run_policy=SKIP, next_run_time=2 days ago, last_run_time=4 days ago. Advance engine's clock to 'today' and trigger missed-run detection. Assert: (1) no backfill tasks were created, (2) job state marked as either skipped or next_run_time jumped forward. Repeat with missed_run_policy=BACKFILL and assert backfill tasks exist for each missed interval (e.g., two tasks for two missed 24-hour windows).
